steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The first clause states the revisions are “extreme, even by James’s standards.” The blank introduces what follows: critics regard the two editions as different novels altogether. The transition must show that the critics’ judgment reinforces — not contradicts or restates — the claim of extremity.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — by contrast,: signals opposition between the two clauses. But both clauses describe the same extremity, so there is no contrast. ✗
  • Choice B — in fact,: signals that what follows validates or intensifies the prior claim. Critics treating the editions as separate novels confirms just how extreme the revisions were. ✓
  • Choice C — nevertheless,: signals concession — that something happens despite the prior condition. The critics’ judgment is not a concession; it reinforces the claim. ✗
  • Choice D — in other words,: signals a restatement in equivalent terms. The critics’ view adds new information rather than rephrasing the same idea. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
